segments.title: Correcting a D-class length error
segments.summary: A follow-up corrects the D-class length figures and shows why even familiar reference books need a second check.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke opens by acknowledging readers' corrections to dimensions used in the earlier D-class discussion. He had initially treated the disagreement as a question of particular subclasses because the reference work was one he had used successfully before. He then returned both to Whitley's cruiser reference and to other books instead of relying on his first explanation. The short correction becomes a practical historical-method lesson: a plausible number still needs an independent check of its units and conversion.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke introduces the correction, describes reader feedback, and explains why he returned to the sources.

segments.title: Checking feet-to-metre conversions
segments.summary: The reference book's feet measurements are sound, but several printed metric conversions are not, demonstrating how a trusted source can still introduce error.
segments.body: The episode pinpoints the problem in the metric conversion rather than the underlying imperial measurements. The printed feet values are sound, but several metre equivalents beside them are wrong. Dr. Clarke notes that the same book had been reliable for other cruiser classes, which made the error easier to miss. He also admits that the mistaken dimensions seemed to fit what he had already read about the ships' speed and hull form. He therefore accepts responsibility for not recalculating the figures from the feet values in front of him.

evidence.note: The transcript identifies correct feet figures alongside faulty metre conversions and explains why the error was not caught earlier.

segments.title: Recalculating the cruiser lengths
segments.summary: A live recalculation revises the comparative lengths used in the earlier episode, including one figure that had been overstated by more than ten metres.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke works through the conversion again and replaces the earlier length comparison with the recalculated result. For the first Danae batch he obtains 143.56 metres, which agrees closely with the book's rounded 143.5-metre value. The comparison figure recalculates to 137.61 metres rather than the printed 148.12 metres, a discrepancy of about eleven metres. That makes the D-class ships roughly seven metres longer than the cruisers being compared, reversing the earlier conclusion. The episode shows why a dimensional error can travel into later claims about hull form and performance rather than remaining a harmless rounding issue.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke recalculates the disputed figures, gives revised lengths, and states that one comparison differed by about eleven metres.

segments.title: Hull form, power, and the speed question
segments.summary: The revised dimensions raise a design question: similar weight and power do not make speed predictable when hull proportions and propellers differ.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke outlines a useful rule of thumb that, for the same horsepower, a longer and narrower hull tends to gain speed. The corrected comparison leaves ships of broadly similar weight with the D class slightly longer and only somewhat wider in beam. On those proportions he might expect about 29.5 knots, yet the cited figure is 29 knots. He treats that mismatch as an opening for investigation rather than a complete explanation of performance. Propeller form is the next variable he identifies for a closer look.

evidence.note: The transcript connects length, beam, weight, horsepower, and speed before identifying propeller shape as a further question.

segments.title: Why corrections belong in teaching
segments.summary: The closing reflection turns reader feedback and a public correction into a lesson in careful historical method.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke thanks the viewers who raised the issue and stresses that their feedback was both observant and constructive. He apologizes for the mistake and presents the exchange as an example of how criticism can improve historical work. He plans to place the correction in both his teaching material and the cruiser section so students can see that lecturers can also be called out when they do not check carefully. The lesson extends beyond cruiser dimensions to the habit of tracing figures to their source and checking them before building an argument on them.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke thanks the commenters and says the correction will be used as a teaching example about checking work.
